title-group.article-title: Attitudes and misconceptions surrounding epilepsy and first aid of seizures in the Eastern Mediterranean Region: a systematic review and meta-analysis

sec.title: Purpose
sec.p: Misconceptions and knowledge gaps regarding epilepsy in the Eastern Mediterranean region (EMR) Arabic-speaking countries are common and may endanger people with epilepsy (PWE). This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to quantify public knowledge, misconceptions, and attitudes and synthesize pooled evidence to guide public health and educational interventions.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This is a systematic review and meta-analysis of misconceptions and attitudes within Arabic-speaking countries regarding epilepsy. A comprehensive literature search of the following databases: PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science were conducted following PRISMA guidelines. Studies were included if they were cross-sectional studies assessing epilepsy awareness and/or attitudes and conducted in Arabic-speaking countries. The Appraisal Tool for Cross-Sectional Studies (AXIS) and the Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) critical appraisal tool were used for quality assessment. Heterogeneity between studies was evaluated using the I² statistic. Results were reported as pooled proportions with a 95% confidence interval (CI). Sensitivity and subgroup analyses assessed how individual studies affected the pooled results. The analysis was performed by Open Meta Analyst.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: As of January 2024, 75 of 5,646 studies satisfied the inclusion criteria with a total sample size of 61,681. The pooled prevalence of good knowledge and positive attitude were 47.2% and 50.5%, respectively. The most common myth about seizure first aid was that placing objects in the mouth will decrease tongue biting with a pooled prevalence of 38.6%. Beliefs attributing the disorder to spirit possession and the evil eye had a pooled prevalence of 14.1% and 17.3%, respectively. Approximately 45% and 62% of participants were uncomfortable with their children playing with or marrying PWE. Contagion beliefs were common in the Levant (29.6%), while spirit possession was prevalent in North Africa (22.1%). Schoolteachers in the EMR had the lowest knowledge (42.2%) and least positive attitudes (33.9%). Pooled knowledge level showed minimal improvement over the past decade.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: There are still a lot of misunderstandings about epilepsy in the EMR, and these may vary depending on the demographic, study area, or historical period. Additionally, stigma around epilepsy remains a significant issue, highlighting the critical need for targeted awareness programs and legislative measures.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12889-025-25510-1.

abstract.title: Key points
abstract.p: • In this systematic review study, many cultural misconceptions surrounding epilepsy were widespread among populations of the Eastern Mediterranean Region.
• There is still a belief that epilepsy is attributed to evil eyes and spirits in the region.
• Wrong-handling seizures, such as restraining the person and placing objects in the mouth, were common in the region.
• Misconceptions and negative attitudes regarding marriage with people with epilepsy are prevalent among nearly half of the participants.
• Focused interventions are essential to address widespread misconceptions and cultural biases, thereby improving the safety of people with epilepsy.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12889-025-25510-1.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Epilepsy is a chronic neurological disorder characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures, affecting 50 million people worldwide [1]. Approximately 80% of people with epilepsy (PWE) live in low- and middle-income countries, where the disease remains a major public health problem, not only because of its health implications but also for its social, cultural, psychological, and economic effects [2–4]. The quality of life of PWE can be significantly impacted by stigma and discrimination, often leading to negative psychological outcomes, social isolation, and decreased opportunities in education and employment [5, 6].

PWE experiences seizures without warning, anywhere and anytime. Thus, appropriate seizure first aid is crucial and can save a life. Unfortunately, there are several common misconceptions and inappropriate measures, such as restraining the person during the seizure, placing objects in the mouth during a seizure to prevent tongue swallowing, or forcing medications down the throat for a PWE, that can cause severe physical injuries or choking leading to death [7].

The Eastern Mediterranean region (EMR) is divided into transcontinental regions that include North Africa, the Levant, and the Gulf Cooperative Council (GCC) countries. Each subregion shares many cultural and linguistic similarities. Arabic is widely spoken across the region, serving as a unifying linguistic factor, while cultural norms, religious practices, and traditional beliefs often overlap. These shared characteristics create a common ground for prevalence and types of epilepsy-related misconceptions. As stigma is widely influenced by the general awareness of epilepsy and the attitudes regarding PWE, it is still a serious issue in the Arabic-speaking EMR countries [7]. Misconceptions about the causes of epilepsy, natural history, treatment, and first aid for a PWE person are widespread, as well as negative attitudes regarding PWE [8].

Many cross-sectional studies have been carried out, revealing prevalent unsatisfactory epilepsy awareness levels and inappropriate seizure responses across many countries in the Arabic-speaking EMR countries, highlighting the urgent need for effective educational interventions. Therefore, the purpose of the present study is to determine and quantify the pooled prevalence of misconceptions and attitude regarding epilepsy within Arabic-speaking EMR countries and to identify key correlates of knowledge and attitude about the condition. The evidence generated from this review aims to guide the creation of evidence-based educational frameworks and public health initiatives, with the goal of diminishing stigma, increasing awareness about epilepsy, and ultimately improving the quality of life of PWE in the region.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: This study is a quantitative systematic review and meta-analysis that follows the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines. The review protocol was registered in the International Prospective Register of Systematic Reviews (PROSPERO; registration ID: CRD42024621092), and it includes review objectives, eligibility criteria, comprehensive search strategy, data extraction procedure, quality appraisal tools, and planned meta-analytic approaches.

sec.title: Criteria of included studies
sec.p: We included studies that met the following criteria:

sec.title: Study design
sec.p: Questionnaire-based cross-sectional studies assessing epilepsy awareness and/or attitudes. This study design was selected as it is the most common and methodologically suitable method for evaluating population-level knowledge and attitudes. This design allows for consistent measurement across various populations and facilitates quantitative aggregation of pooled proportions, improving the comparability of studies included in the meta-analysis.

sec.title: Population
sec.p: Studies conducted in Arabic-speaking countries targeting the general population, patients with epilepsy, caregivers of PWE, university students (both medical and non-medical), schoolteachers, and healthcare professionals.

sec.title: Time frame
sec.p: Studies conducted at any time, with no limitations on publication date.

sec.title: Language
sec.p: Studies published in either English or Arabic.

sec.title: Definitions
sec.p: Epilepsy awareness is defined as understanding the existence of the condition, its causes, symptoms, management strategies, progression, prognosis, treatments, and curability. Attitudes regarding epilepsy are described as an individual’s opinions about PWE, including how PWE should act within the community and how the community should respond to them.

sec.title: Outcomes measures
sec.p: In this systematic review and meta-analysis, the primary outcomes were the levels of awareness and attitudes regarding epilepsy among the study populations in the included cross-sectional studies. Awareness was measured as knowledge of epilepsy, including understanding of its causes, symptoms, treatment options, prognosis, and general disease familiarity. Attitude was assessed based on participants’ beliefs, and opinions regarding PWE and how they should be treated within the community. Data on each outcome were extracted in proportions to allow for standardized comparison across studies. Studies reporting these outcomes in terms of “yes/no” or “agree/disagree” responses were included. For pooled estimates calculations, only the number of yes responses and the total sample from each study were extracted. Pooled estimates were produced for all major outcomes in the meta-analysis, while simple proportions were only given for descriptive parameters like demographics.

We excluded studies that met any of the following criteria: (1) conducted outside of Arabic-speaking countries, (2) not employing a cross-sectional design, (3) studies without accessible full text, (4) not reporting outcomes on epilepsy awareness or attitudes in proportions, and (5) published in languages other than English or Arabic.

sec.title: Literature search and keywords
sec.p: An electronic search was performed in PubMed, Scopus, and Web of Science, covering records from the beginning of each database up to January 2024. For a sensitive search strategy, we used MESH keywords. To enhance the accuracy and relevance of our search results, we employed Boolean operators, specifically AND and OR. The primary keywords were (Arab countries) AND ((epilepsy) OR (seizure) OR (convulsion)) AND ((knowledge) OR (awareness) OR (attitudes)). The search strategy is shown in a supplementary file. Additionally, a snowball search was performed to identify any potentially missing studies by examining reference lists of publications eligible for full-text review and systematic review articles on knowledge and attitudes regarding epilepsy in any Arab countries in January 2024.

sec.title: Screening and study selection process
sec.p: We used Rayyan for semi-automated screening of the literature search results [9]. The screening process for studies was conducted in two stages. In the first stage, titles and abstracts were screened, followed by a second stage where full-text articles of the selected abstracts were reviewed for eligibility. The literature search and screening were carried out independently by two review authors, with any disagreements resolved through the opinion of a senior reviewer.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: The studies in this review asked various questions to participants, necessitating a systematic process to identify and group similar questions and quantify the frequency of each question. Initially, we examined a random subset of 20 studies to identify questions, resulting in 48 unique questions across the studies. Any additional questions encountered during data extraction that were not included in the original data collection sheet were added. Since question-wording differed across studies, questions with English synonyms sharing identical Arabic translations were included, as well as questions addressing the same concept (e.g., “Have you heard about epilepsy” vs. “Have you heard/read about fits”) were treated as equivalent, with their outcomes reported together. The proportion of positive responses (e.g., “yes,” “agree with the statement,” etc.) was recorded. Only questions appearing in at least three studies were included, and results are presented as ranges from the lowest to highest proportion of positive responses.

The data was extracted via an online shared spreadsheet, which had been piloted prior to data extraction. Two independent reviewers were responsible for data collection from each included report. Reviewers worked independently during the data collection phase. Extracted data were compared, with any discrepancies being resolved through discussion.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The quality of the included studies was evaluated using two tools including the Appraisal Tool for Cross-Sectional Studies (AXIS), and Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) critical appraisal tool [10, 11]. The AXIS tool addresses major aspects of study design, sampling, measurement validity, and ethical considerations. The JBI checklist has eight elements that assess the clarity of inclusion criteria, measurement validity, confounder control, and appropriateness of statistical analyses.

These tools each capture distinct methodological elements to ensure a comprehensive evaluation. Each study was independently evaluated by two reviewers, and any disagreements were discussed and resolved, or a third reviewer was consulted when necessary (Supplementary file 2). The ROBVIS tool was used to create risk-of-bias plots for quality assessment. Both the domain-specific evaluations and the overall risk of bias across all included studies were summarized and visually represented with a traffic light map [12].

sec.title: Data synthesis and meta-analysis
sec.p: Open Meta Analyst was used for statistical analyses (CEBM, University of Oxford, Oxford, UK). Only questions that had to be consistently reported in at least three research studies were incorporated into the meta-analysis. A random-effects model was utilized to accommodate variability across studies, with outcomes expressed as proportions with a 95% confidence interval (CI). To quantify the extent of heterogeneity attributable to differences between studies, the I² statistic was applied, with a P-value of 0.05 serving as the threshold for significance. Findings are presented in summary tables and forest plots. A leave-one-out method was used to conduct sensitivity analyses in order to assess the results’ robustness. Publication bias testing using funnel plots and related tests was not performed since they did not yield reliable findings for meta-analysis of proportions [13].

A subgroup analysis was conducted to explore differences in attitudes and knowledge regarding epilepsy. The studies were categorized by geographic region (North Africa, the Gulf, and the Levant) and study year (2001–2014 vs. 2015–2024), with a classification for 10-year and 15-year intervals. Furthermore, subgroup analyses were performed based on study population (general population, schoolteachers, university students, caregivers of individuals with epilepsy, and healthcare professionals). To find trends and differences across various demographic and geographic parameters, pooled values for knowledge and attitude levels were computed for each subgroup (Tables 3, 4 and 5).

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Extracted data were mainly divided into domains: (1) study characteristics, including study ID, year, country, target population, sample size, sampling technique, and percentage of males in the sample (Table 1); and (2) the categories of different questions. Then we grouped questions into eleven different categories: questions assessing familiarity with epilepsy (n = 6), knowledge about seizure symptoms (n = 3), receiving first aid training either in general or specific seizure first aid (n = 2), knowledge about seizure first aid (n = 7), knowledge about causes of epilepsy (n = 10), knowledge about epilepsy treatment (n = 8), attitudes regarding children with epilepsy (n = 3), attitudes regarding employment (n = 2), attitudes regarding marriage (n = 3), attitudes regarding social activities (n = 2), and attitudes regarding personal relationships (n = 2).Table 1

sec.title: Characteristics of included studies
sec.p: Our comprehensive search yielded 5,639 studies. After removing duplicates and screening titles and abstracts, 185 articles were assessed for eligibility based on the inclusion criteria. In the final phase, the systematic review covered 75 studies conducted between 2008 and 2024, except for one study done in 2001 (Fig. 1).

caption.p: Flow chart depicting the selection process of the included studies

sec.p: The studies were conducted across 14 different countries. Saudi Arabia was the highest contributor, with 44 studies that were conducted. Jordan conducted five studies, followed by Egypt, which contributed four studies. Sudan, Lebanon, and Morocco each had three studies. Bahrain, Iraq, Kuwait, Palestine, and Tunisia each contributed two studies. Libya, Oman, and Yemen each had one study.

The target populations in the included studies varied widely. The largest group studied were schoolteachers, represented in 26 studies (34.7%). The general population was the focus of 24 studies (32%), followed by medical university students in 9 studies (12%) and non-medical university students in 3 studies (4%). Caregivers of PWE were included in 4 studies (5.3%), while health care professionals were targeted in 2 studies (2.7%). The studies focused on smaller groups, including physical educators, school students, and caregivers of school students (1.3% each) (Table 1).

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: For quality assessment, most studies have adequate quality according to AXIS, no high risk of bias was identified. Concerns only arise in measures undertaken to address and categories non-responders’ domain. However, risk of bias is identified in only two studies as revealed by JBI. All studies were kept to ensure comprehensive representativeness of literature The effect of research quality on pooled estimates was investigated using sensitivity and subgroup analyses, and the results were consistent, indicating that the main conclusions are robust and consistent. (Fig. 2, Supplementary file 2).

caption.p: a RobVis (risk of bias visualization) plot using the Appraisal Tool for Cross-Sectional Studies (AXIS tool), (b) RobVis (risk of bias visualization) plot using Joanna Briggs Institute (JBI) checklist

sec.title: Pooled knowledge of epilepsy
sec.p: A total of 22 studies, with about 23,693 participants, reported the level of good knowledge about epilepsy. According to the random effect model, good knowledge regarding epilepsy was determined in 47.2% (95% CI: 36.9%−57.5%) (Fig. 3). The leave-one-out sensitivity analysis supported the accuracy of the knowledge estimates, as no single study significantly changed the overall pooled prevalence (Fig. 4). This indicates that the findings are reliable and unaffected by any particular study.

caption.p: Forest plot of prevalence of good knowledge about epilepsy
A-leave-out -one sensitivity analysis for knowledge regarding epilepsy

sec.p: Subgroup analysis revealed varying levels of epilepsy knowledge, with some groups demonstrating a better understanding of the condition. Regional differences in knowledge were noticed. The highest pooled knowledge level was found by studies done in the Levant (89.8%; 95% CI: 85.8%−93.2%), followed by North Africa (69.3%; 95% CI: 57.7%−80.9%) (Table 3). University students had the highest knowledge levels (62.7%; 95% CI: 32.7%−92.8%), followed by the general population (52.3%), while schoolteachers had lower knowledge levels (42.2%) according to stratification by study population (Table 3). Research from 2001 to 2014 reported an estimated knowledge level of 46% (95% CI: 8.9%−83.2%), while research from 2015 to 2024 reported a slight increase in the estimated knowledge level to 47.4% (95% CI: 36.5%−58.3%). (Table 3). Overall, knowledge levels varied across regions, being lowest among schoolteachers, though a gradual improvement was apparent over time.

sec.title: Familiarity about epilepsy
sec.p: Three questions (Q7-Q9) related to knowledge about seizures were included in the meta-analysis. The results showed that 72.7% of the population (95% CI: 64.4%−81%) identified seizures by convulsions or shaking. In contrast, only 34.5% (95% CI: 23.5%−45.4%) believed that behavioral changes could also be a manifestation of a seizure. Furthermore, 48.2% of participants (95% CI: 37.3%−59.2%) reported knowing how to help a PWE during a seizure (Table 2).

sec.title: Seizures first aid
sec.p: Two questions assess whether participants have first aid training or not (Q10-Q11). The proportion of participants who had first aid training is estimated to be 3.7% (CI 2.9%−4.5%). However, 18.5% (CI 12.2%−24.8%) of participants were trained in seizure first aid (Table 2).

The meta-analysis assessed seven questions (Q12-Q18) regarding knowledge of seizure first aid. It was found that 24.7% of participants (95% CI: 19.2%−30.3%) believed restraining the patient’s movements is a correct first aid measure during a seizure. Meanwhile, 37.1% (95% CI: 21.3%−52.9%) considered using eau de cologne or onion for smelling as a first aid response. Additionally, 38.6% of participants (95% CI: 27.3%−50%) thought placing something in the patient’s mouth to prevent tongue biting was appropriate. The proportion of participants who would remove the patient from danger for safety reasons during a seizure was estimated to be 59.2% (95% CI: 45%−73.3%), while only 4.4% (95% CI: 1.3%−7.5%) indicated they would place the patient’s head in the toilet. Furthermore, 17.1% (95% CI: 8.7%−25.6%) believed forcing medication into the patient’s throat was a first aid response. Lastly, 30.6% (95% CI: 21.7%−39.5%) admitted they were unsure how to respond during a seizure (Table 2). In summary, most studies reported insufficient training in seizure first aid, with notable gap in understanding of correct first aid procedures during seizures.

sec.title: Knowledge about causes
sec.p: The meta-analysis included ten questions (Q19-Q28) exploring participants’ beliefs about the causes of epilepsy. It was found that 17.5% of participants (95% CI: 11.2%−24.2%) believed epilepsy is a contagious disease. Meanwhile, 44.7% (95% CI: 37.3%−52.2%) identified epilepsy as a hereditary condition. The proportion of participants who attributed epilepsy to birth defects was 25.2% (95% CI: 20.9%−29.5%). A significant 66.9% (95% CI: 56.7%−77.1%) identified epilepsy as brain or neurological disorder. However, only 5.8% (95% CI: 4.1%−7.5%) thought epilepsy was a blood disorder. Additionally, 30.2% (95% CI: 24.4%−36%) believed that fever or inflammation could cause convulsions, while 29.3% (95% CI: 24.2%−34.4%) considered epilepsy to be a psychological disorder stemming from mental or emotional stress. Furthermore, 9.7% (95% CI: 7.8%−11.6%) associated epilepsy with insanity, and 14.1% (95% CI: 11.6%−16.6%) attributed it to spirit possession (jinn). Lastly, 17.3% (95% CI: 13%−21.7%) believed that epilepsy is caused by the evil eye (envy) (Table 2).

By region, Levant countries think the most that epilepsy is contagious (29.6%), while the Gulf countries are the lowest (14.1%). Similarly, the highest percentage of people who think epilepsy is hereditary was found in Levant countries (47.2%), and the lowest was in North African countries (35%). Correct awareness of epilepsy as a neurological disorder is highest among the Levant countries (82.9%), followed by the Gulf countries (60.7%). Nearly one-third of the participants who studied in North Africa, the Gulf, and the Levant believe that epilepsy is a psychological disorder (30%, 26.4%, and 31.3%, respectively). North Africa had the highest percentage of participants with the incorrect notion that spirit possession caused epilepsy at 22.1%, followed by the Gulf and the Levant (11.9% and 8.5%, respectively) (Table 4). Over time, there is only a slight improvement in correct knowledge about causes of epilepsy (Table 4).

Based on the study population, the general population was the highest to believe that epilepsy is contagious (17.7%), while caregivers of school students were the lowest (0.2%). A notably high percentage of health care professionals and physical educators believe that epilepsy is hereditary (88.9% and 80.1%, respectively). The neurological nature of epilepsy was confirmed by 95.3% of healthcare professionals and only 63% of the general population. No obvious difference was found between different study populations regarding the psychological nature of epilepsy. However, caregivers of PWE and the general population were the most likely to belief that epilepsy is caused by spirit possession (43.9% and 17.2% respectively) (Table 4). Overall, misconceptions linking epilepsy to contagion and supernatural causes persisted particularly among general population, despite regional and temporal variations.

sec.title: Knowledge about treatment
sec.p: Eight questions assessing participants’ knowledge of epilepsy treatment were included in the meta-analysis (Q29-Q36). The results showed that 64.5% of participants (95% CI: 53.9%−75.2%) believed epilepsy is a treatable condition. However, 20.6% (95% CI: 14.1%−27.1%) thought there was no need for treatment. A total of 57.1% (95% CI: 44%−70.2%) believed that medical treatment is available and effective, while 23.3% (95% CI: 18.9%−27.7%) considered surgery as a possible treatment option for epilepsy. Furthermore, 18.4% (95% CI: 13.6%−23.1%) believed that herbal medicine could be used to treat epilepsy, and 10.2% (95% CI: 5.5%−14.9%) thought acupuncture was a viable treatment option. Additionally, 25.8% (95% CI: 12.3%−39.4%) considered cauterization as a treatment, and 39.9% (95% CI: 22.3%−37.5%) believed that the Quran or a spiritual healer could offer treatment for epilepsy (Table 2).

Moreover, knowledge regarding epilepsy management also demonstrated regional variation. North Africa had the highest percentage of respondents who believed that epilepsy was a treatable condition (80.5%), followed by the Gulf (62.7%) and the Levant (57.7%). About half of the general population respondents believed that epilepsy is a treatable disease (49.2%). However, higher percentages were noted among university students and schoolteachers (70.5% and 63.1%, respectively) (Table 4).

sec.title: Pooled positive attitude regarding epilepsy
sec.p: Ten studies explored attitudes regarding epilepsy, and the pooled positive attitude was determined in only half of participants (50.5% (CI: 30.3% −70.8%))(Fig. 5). The findings’ reliability was further confirmed by the leave-one-out sensitivity analysis for attitudes regarding epilepsy. There was no significant change in the pooled prevalence when individual studies were excluded, suggesting a high level of consistency (Fig. 6).

caption.p: Forest plot of prevalence of favorable attitude about epilepsy
A-leave-out -one sensitivity analysis for attitude regarding epilepsy

sec.p:
When classified by region, studies from North Africa showed the highest positive attitudes (80.9%; 95% CI: 74.3%−87.6%), followed by the Gulf region (44.4%; 95% CI: 19.6%−69.2%), whereas Levant countries had lower acceptance (33.3%). There were also obvious variations depending on the study population. The most favorable attitudes were shown by the general population (74.3%) and university students (55.2%; 95% CI: 12.3%−98.2%). At 33.9%, schoolteachers had the least positive sentiments (Table 3).Table 4

Over the past decade, the pooled estimate for favorable attitudes was 50.5% (95% CI: 30.3%−70.8%), indicating an increase in acceptability. However, estimating the level of favorable attitude wasn’t an area of interest when extended to a 15-year period (2001–2014), demonstrating the need for continuous efforts to increase acceptance and lessen stigma. Overall, about half of the population demonstrated a positive attitude regarding epilepsy, with schoolteachers showing the least positive attitudes. Research on attitudes regarding epilepsy has largely emerged only within the past decade.

sec.title: Attitudes regarding children with epilepsy
sec.p: The meta-analysis included three questions assessing attitudes regarding children with epilepsy (Q37-Q39). It was found that 63.7% of participants (95% CI: 53.4%−73.9%) agreed that children with epilepsy can be successful in a normal class. However, 50.2% (95% CI: 33.1%−67.3%) believed that children with epilepsy have below-normal intelligence. Additionally, 55.2% of participants (95% CI: 44.5%−66%) were comfortable with their child playing with a friend who has epilepsy (Table 2).

In the subgroup analysis, 52% of participants from North Africa agreed that their children play with a friend who has epilepsy, compared to 50.7% from the Gulf and 70.4% from the Levant (Table 5). This attitude shows a time trend; there is a decrease in the pooled prevalence of people who accept their children playing with a PWE from 60% between 2001 and 2014 to 54.2% between 2015 and 2024. Also, about half of the general population and schoolteachers (54.3% and 57.5%, respectively) approve of their children playing with a friend with epilepsy (Table 5).Table 5

sec.title: Attitudes regarding PWE
sec.p: Two questions (Q40-Q41) regarding attitudes regarding PWE were included in the meta-analysis. A total of 56% of participants (95% CI: 43.8%−68.3%) believed that PWE can be employed like anyone else. Additionally, 70.7% (95% CI: 59.9%−81.6%) felt it is acceptable to work alongside a colleague with epilepsy (Table 2). Positive attitude regarding employment of PWE is higher in North African countries (62.8%) and lowest in Levant countries (46.3%). No significant change was observed in this attitude over time. Health care professionals and university students were the highest to exhibit this mindset (70.6% and 64.4%, respectively) (Table 5).

sec.title: Attitudes regarding marriage
sec.p: Three questions regarding attitudes regarding marriage PWE were included in the meta-analysis (Q42-Q44). Although 76.4% (CI 62.9%−89.9%) and 73% (CI 60.4%−85.7%) of participants were estimated to think PWE can marry normally and can have children, respectively, only 37.9% (CI 29.5%−46.4%) of them would approve if their son/daughter married a PWE (Table 2).

According to the subgroup analysis, 73.6% of participants from North Africa believed PWE could marry normally, compared to 75.4% from the Gulf and 90.4% from the Levant. The attitude regarding marriage of PWE showed a clear upward tendency over time, rising from 55.2% in research conducted between 2001 and 2014 to 80.4% in studies conducted between 2015 and 2024. No obvious difference was revealed in the pooled prevalence of attitude regarding PWE marriage between the general population, schoolteachers, and university students (74%, 83.6%, and 84.4%, respectively) (Table 5).

sec.title: Attitudes regarding social activities and personal relationships
sec.p: Two questions (Q45-Q46) assessing attitudes regarding social activities for PWE were included in the meta-analysis. A total of 39% (95% CI: 26.3%−51.7%) felt that PWE should not engage in sports activities, while 37.7% of participants (95% CI: 27.3%−48.1%) believed that PWE are able to drive normally (Table 2).

Regarding geographic variations, 41.4% of participants from North Africa, 42% from the Gulf, and only 18.7% from the Levant agreed that PWE can drive normally (Table 5). In studies conducted between 2001 and 2014, only 24% accepted that PWE can drive normally compared to 41.4% in studies between 2015 and 2024 (Table 5).

The meta-analysis included two questions evaluating attitudes regarding personal relationships with PWE (Q47-Q48). It was found that 58% of participants (95% CI: 36.1%−79.9%) were open to becoming close friends with someone who has epilepsy. Additionally, 76.9% of participants (95% CI: 63.8%−95.4%) would treat a PWE the same as they did before (Table 2). Based on subgroup analysis, 77.4% of Gulf participants and 97.3% of Levant participants said they would treat PWE the same way as before. Also, schoolteachers were the most likely to exhibit this positive attitude (94%) (Table 5). Overall, the pooled findings indicate that negative attitudes remain common, particularly concerning marriage, driving and employment of people with epilepsy.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This review presents the first comprehensive regional assessment of public knowledge regarding epilepsy and attitudes regarding PWE across different Arabic-speaking countries in the EMR. The study explores how people understand epilepsy, its causes, symptoms, and treatments, as well as practical aspects such as seizure management and first aid. It also examines attitudes regarding PWE, especially in social and personal settings like education, employment, and relationships. The findings, presented as general percentages, emphasize ongoing knowledge deficiencies and prevalent misconceptions, underscoring areas where more education and awareness could make a real difference.

The analysis draws from studies across 14 Arabic-speaking EMR countries, offering a wide-ranging view that includes diverse groups and experiences. Compared with earlier reviews that were limited to individual countries or small-scale studies, this broader scope allows for a more detailed understanding of how epilepsy is perceived in different communities. However, most of the original studies are from Gulf countries, with fewer from North Africa and the Levant. This geographical discrepancy highlights the necessity of more regional representation from under-studied regions.

Despite epilepsy being one of the most common neurological disorders, the current meta-analysis revealed that good knowledge was found in only less than half of the studied population (47.2%). This is consistent with a meta-analysis conducted in Ethiopia (51.5%) [90]. Moreover, a survey conducted in the US revealed suboptimal knowledge of epilepsy among the US public [91]. These results indicate a lack of adequate correct knowledge about epilepsy. Insufficient knowledge and false beliefs could be the root cause of persistent stigma, social exclusion, and delayed treatment-seeking behaviors [92].

Subgroup analyses offer valuable insights on the variables affecting understanding of epilepsy. Knowledge levels varied markedly by subregion, with the Levant showing the highest awareness levels (89.8%), followed by North Africa (69.3%) and the Gulf region (40%). However, the better knowledge revealed from the Levant and North Africa may be influenced by fewer studies from these areas than Gulf countries. The observed variation underscores the importance of culturally tailored awareness programs and the inclusion of underrepresented populations in future studies [93].

Further analysis over time revealed that good knowledge has marginally increased over the past ten years (from 46% between 2001 and 2014 to 47.4% from 2015 to 2024), suggesting that progress has been limited despite advances in health communication. A prior analysis of a decade’s research conducted between 2000 and 2010 revealed that although awareness increased, notable knowledge and attitude disparities remained [94]. This stagnation highlights the necessity of ongoing, targeted educational initiatives focusing on practical seizure management and dispelling myths.

The results found that almost 87.9% of participants knew something about epilepsy, with 66.7% claiming to have a good grasp of what epilepsy is, 41.5% said they knew someone with epilepsy. Another cross-sectional study conducted among 964 random people in Thailand states that only 60% and 12.3% of participants had heard about epilepsy and knew someone with epilepsy, respectively [93]. According to a prior multicenter survey, familiarity about epilepsy was generally low, ​with 38.9% of Portuguese students reported helping someone in a convulsive crisis, followed by students from Argentina (26.7%), Brazil (24.4%), and the USA (10.7%) [95]. While numbers in Arabic-speaking EMR countries are promising, they moreover highlight gaps in knowledge, especially when it comes to knowledge about causes, treatment and dealing with seizures, suggesting that hearing about the condition is insufficient to ensure correct knowledge and accurate understanding of epilepsy.

In this study, 59.2% of participants detailed that they would take action to remove the patient from potential threat during a seizure for security reasons, while only 3.7% received formal first aid training. Despite some awareness, several misconceptions about epilepsy first aid persist. Notably, 24.7% of participants believed that restraining the patient’s movements during a seizure was appropriate first aid, and 17.2% thought forcing medication into the patient’s throat was helpful. Comparable findings were revealed in previous studies. In Tehran, it was found that 70% of participants attempted to stop someone with active seizure from falling or getting harmed; however, less than 20% took them to the hospital [96]. In China, only 9.3% of participants had correct understanding of seizures first aid [97]. Significant gaps were also observed in the epilepsy first-aid knowledge of Grenadian individuals. For example, 17.7% of respondents thought that medication should be put in the victim’s mouth during a seizure [98]. Such data supports the need for standardizing training in seizure first aid across different populations.

Misconceptions about etiology were also common, with 17.5% thinking it was contagious, primarily in Levant nations (29.5%). Additionally, 14.1% of people, especially in North Africa (22.1%), believe it to be spirit possession. About 40% believed that spiritual or Quranic healing could effectively treat epilepsy, reflecting the influence of traditional beliefs. General public studies identify more widespread misunderstandings compared to other research populations, including the notion that epilepsy is contagious (17.7%), a psychiatric condition (25.5%), and caused by spirits (17.2%), while only half (49.2%) recognized it as a curable condition. By dispelling these myths and educating people about epilepsy, especially in the Levant and North Africa, as well as the broader public, including the fact that it is not communicable or the result of spirit possession and that it could be managed with appropriate medications, the stigma associated with the condition may be mitigated, encouraging people to seek medical advice and thus improve the quality of life of PWE.

Similar cultural patterns have been reported globally. For instance, in Tehran, where 29.6% of 883 participants believed insanity was a cause of epilepsy, and 15.7% attributed it to the “evil eye” [96]. A study in Ethiopia also found that nearly half (46.6%) of 596 participants believed epilepsy was caused by an evil spirit [99]. Another study conducted in Thailand states that almost 80% of the participants did not know the causes of epilepsy, with one-fifth believing that epilepsy is caused by evil, while two-fifths thought it was due to eating pork. Additionally, about 20% of the participants trusted spiritual healers as a treatment for epilepsy [93]. This contrasts with a study among students in Rome, Italy, where 71% identified epilepsy as a neurological disorder [100]. Robust health education programs and increased exposure to scientific data in academic contexts may all contribute to Italy’s students’ higher level of proper information.

Overall, only half of the Arabic-speaking EMR population (50.5%) demonstrated a positive attitude regarding PWE. This result is consistent with Ethiopian meta-analysis research, which exhibited that a favorable attitude regarding PWE was found in less than half of participants (48%) [101]. Similarly, another meta-analysis showed that only 55.5% of the Ethiopian population don’t show stigma against epilepsy patients [102]. Studies from China [103], Turkey [104] and Thailand [105] confirmed suboptimal attitudes regarding epilepsy. Negative societal attitudes are the root cause of the stigma associated with epilepsy. It is advised that in order to lessen prejudice, false impressions, and discrimination against epilepsy, positive attitudes be promoted.

Regional differences in attitudes regarding PWE were found to be significant. North Africa, for example, had the highest percentage of positive acts (80.9%), while the Gulf area and the Levant had lower percentages (44.4% and 33.3%, respectively). This suggests regional differences in how epilepsy is understood and accepted. Moreover, studies that emphasize attitude level were found to be the key area of research in the last decade, indicating that attitude regarding epilepsy and stigma have become an important research focus. A previous German study evaluating attitudes regarding epilepsy over the past five decades, showed that significant progress has been made in lowering social distance and negative stereotypes. However, emotional responses like fear and worries about encountering PWE still require attention, suggesting the need to develop new approaches to address the negative attitude regarding epilepsy globally [106].

A large majority, about 76.9%, reported they would treat PWE like anyone else. However, only 58% expressed a willingness to build close friendships with those affected, indicating lingering social reservations.

When it came to employment, just over half (56%) had a favorable view of job opportunities for people with epilepsy. However, acceptance declined further in personal relationships, with only 37.9% agreeing with the idea of a family member marrying someone with epilepsy. Contrasting these findings, a study conducted in Ethiopia revealed a different view. Around 41% of respondents had negative attitudes regarding epilepsy, while only 16% preferred their children avoid contact with individuals affected by the condition [99]. Similarly, another study revealed that more than 20% and 12.9% o participants would not allow their offspring to marry or play with a child with epilepsy [93]. Furthermore, research from Nigeria focusing on teachers rendered a concerning result, as 62% of educators viewed students with epilepsy unfavorably, highlighting significant stigma in educational settings [107]. This indicates the persistance of societal stigma regarding people with epilepsy, which extends into family, social, and educational environments where prejudice and misunderstandings are still prevalent.

In the Gulf and North Africa regions, the prevalence of people who permit their children to play with peers who have epilepsy was only 50.7% and 52%, respectively. That means only one in two people would accept interaction with children with epilepsy. Moreover, in Levant countries, only 46% accept PWE employment. This negative attitude didn’t differ among populations from India [108] and the UK [109]. Moreover, studies including public and schoolteachers revealed unfavorable attitudes regarding acceptance of children with epilepsy engagement, PWE employment, driving a car, or having normal personal relationships. A point of concern is the negative attitude of schoolteachers regarding epilepsy. A previous systematic review confirmed that teachers had higher negative opinions about epilepsy than other conditions particularly regarding playing sports or engaging in physical activities [110]. These inadequacies in schoolteachers’ attitudes frequently resulted in insufficient support for students with epilepsy and promoted stigma. Importantly, educational interventions targeting teachers could considerably enhance their understanding and attitudes, boosting the support provided to students with epilepsy.

The findings of this review provide strong evidence about epilepsy knowledge and attitudes across Eastern Mediterranean countries, including evaluation of their subregions and different study populations. Such findings offer a guide for policy makers and researchers to identify the knowledge gap for educational interventions and areas of research gap to improve knowledge and attitude regarding epilepsy, thus reducing stigma and improving aspects of quality of life of PWE.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: While this meta-analysis provides valuable insights into epilepsy-related knowledge and attitudes across 14 Arabic-speaking EMR countries, several limitations should be considered. First, the significant heterogeneity observed reflects methodological variations in study designs, sampling approaches, and assessment tools. Statistical approaches were used to identify sources of heterogeneity; however, some confounding variables could be missed. Second, this review focused exclusively on quantitative cross-sectional studies, which limits the ability to infer causality or explore contextual factors underlying the observed attitudes and misconceptions. The incorporation of qualitative evidence could have provided richer, deeper, more detailed insights into the cultural and psychosocial factors. Third, the predominance of urban and highly educated participants in included studies may limit generalizability to rural and displaced populations, where traditional healing beliefs persist.

To address these limitations, future research should adopt validated, culturally adapted assessment tools and report key sociodemographic variables consistently. Targeted studies among high-impact groups like schoolteachers, as well as vulnerable populations, including refugees and low-literacy populations, are particularly warranted. Establishing a regional collaborative network would help harmonize study methodologies, promote data comparability, and facilitate the translation of research findings into public health action. Such efforts with culturally sensitive clinical training and community-based awareness programs will be essential to reduce stigma and improve epilepsy outcomes across the Arabic-speaking EMR countries.

sec.title: Summary and conclusion
sec.p: Misconceptions about epilepsy are still high in the Arabic-speaking EMR countries, such as the link between epilepsy and supernatural beliefs and improper first-aid procedures that could put PWE at risk. These misconceptions could be affected by variations in region, population or time. Furthermore, negative attitudes regarding epilepsy continue to be a major problem, impacting social integration, marital opportunities, and employment of people with epilepsy.

Educational programs and focused awareness campaigns, particularly for influential groups including schoolteachers, are necessary to solve these problems. Eliminating myths, raising public awareness of managing epilepsy, and improving general attitudes about epilepsy should be the goals of these campaigns. Moreover, unifying a tool for assessment of knowledge and attitude regarding epilepsy is needed to understand the actual awareness level and attitude in different countries. Providing high-quality research about the topic, particularly from countries with a lack of research data, is required.

ack.title: Acknowledgements
ack.p: We would like to thank the ILAE Eastern Mediterranean executive board (ILAR EMR) and its president Ghaieb Aljandeel, for supporting the initiatives of the Young Epilepsy Section - Eastern Mediterranean Region. We are deeply appreciative of Dr. Ismail A. Ibrahim (Faculty of Health Sciences, Fenerbahce University, Istanbul, Turkey - Danish Epilepsy Centre, Dianalund, Denmark - Global Alliance of Young Researchers) for his crucial advice during this study.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: All authors gave approval to the final version of the manuscript after reviewing the results and accept its submission to the journal. SS contributed to conceptualization, study protocol, writing-review and editing of the manuscript, and supervision. LAM was responsible for administration, methodology, and writing, review and editing of the manuscript. MSR, MND, ER, DAA, AHA, MKA, and ZSA participated in data curation, writing and original draft preparation. SM contributed to formal analysis, visualization, validation, methodology, data curation, and review and editing of the manuscript.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The datasets used during the current study are available from the corresponding author on reasonable request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: The guidelines of the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA statement) were followed when writing this manuscript with PROSPERO registration code CRD42024621092.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
